heroOverview: Hyperprolactinemia is a persistent elevation of prolactin above the normal upper limit of roughly 25 ng/mL in women and 20 ng/mL in men, caused most often by a benign prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma) or by medications that block dopamine signalling. Prolactin is the pituitary hormone that drives lactation, and its excess suppresses the normal pulsatile release of gonadotropin-releasing hormone, interrupting the reproductive axis and causing menstrual irregularity, infertility, and reduced sex hormone levels in both sexes. Population surveys estimate prevalence at 0.4% in unselected adults and up to 17% among women with menstrual disturbances. The condition is highly treatable: dopamine agonist drugs normalise prolactin in more than 80–90% of patients, shrink most tumours measurably, and restore fertility.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 0.4% of the general adult population; up to 17% of women presenting with menstrual disorders
keyStats.demographics: Women aged 20–50 most commonly affected; prolactinomas are the most frequent functional pituitary tumour in both sexes
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in reproductive-age women, 20–40 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 40 million people affected worldwide based on prevalence extrapolation

definition: Hyperprolactinemia (ICD-10: E22.1) is the state of chronically elevated serum prolactin, the hormone synthesised by lactotroph cells in the anterior pituitary gland. Prolactin secretion is tonically suppressed by hypothalamic dopamine; anything that reduces dopamine's inhibitory effect or directly stimulates lactotrophs raises prolactin. The most common pathological cause is a prolactinoma — a benign adenoma of lactotroph cells — which is further classified as a microprolactinoma (diameter <10 mm) or macroprolactinoma (≥10 mm). Non-tumoural causes include medications (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone, some antidepressants), primary hypothyroidism — which raises thyrotropin-releasing hormone and secondarily stimulates prolactin — polycystic ovary syndrome, chronic kidney disease, and chest wall stimulation. Physiological hyperprolactinemia occurs in pregnancy, breastfeeding, and following nipple stimulation, stress, or vigorous exercise. The clinical consequence of pathological elevation is suppression of gonadotropin-releasing hormone pulsatility, which reduces luteinising hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone and thereby lowers oestrogen in women and testosterone in men. This mechanism underlies the reproductive and bone-density consequences that make treatment important even when a tumour is small.

typesClassification.description: A prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma smaller than 10 mm; the most frequent cause in young women, usually producing modest prolactin elevations and managed effectively with oral dopamine agonists.

A prolactinoma 10 mm or larger that can compress adjacent pituitary tissue, the optic chiasm, and the cavernous sinus, producing headaches, visual field loss, and partial hypopituitarism in addition to prolactin excess.

Elevated prolactin caused by drugs that block dopamine D2 receptors (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone) or deplete dopamine stores; levels typically fall within days of stopping the offending drug.
Primary hypothyroidism raises thyrotropin-releasing hormone, which stimulates prolactin alongside TSH; levels normalise with adequate thyroid hormone replacement.
Elevated prolactin with no identifiable tumour on imaging and no secondary cause; it often represents a microprolactinoma too small to visualise, and many cases resolve spontaneously.

primarySymptoms: Menstrual irregularity in women — ranging from oligomenorrhea (infrequent periods) to complete amenorrhea — reflecting prolactin-mediated suppression of ovulation and oestrogen production.
Galactorrhea, the spontaneous or expressible milky discharge from one or both breasts outside of pregnancy or breastfeeding, occurring in up to 80% of women with hyperprolactinemia.
Infertility in women, resulting from anovulation caused by suppressed luteinising hormone surges; this is one of the most common correctable causes of female infertility.
Reduced libido in both men and women, driven by low sex hormone levels secondary to suppressed gonadotropin secretion.
Erectile dysfunction and reduced sperm count in men, caused by hypogonadotropic hypogonadism; this presentation is less recognised but common in affected men.
Low bone mineral density and fragility fractures from prolonged oestrogen or testosterone deficiency; vertebral and hip bone loss accumulates gradually if the condition goes untreated.
Headache, typically frontal or bitemporal, associated with larger tumours or rapid tumour expansion; it does not correlate closely with prolactin level.
Visual field defects — classically bitemporal hemianopia — when a macroprolactinoma compresses the optic chiasm; patients may notice difficulty seeing peripherally without recognising the pattern.
Acne and hirsutism in some women, reflecting the hormonal imbalance produced by anovulation and low oestrogen.
Breast tenderness and engorgement in both sexes from the direct prolactin effect on breast tissue.
earlyWarningSigns: A single missed period or persistent oligomenorrhea in a woman not on hormonal contraception.
Unexplained milky nipple discharge in any non-pregnant, non-breastfeeding adult.
Reduced sex drive that has gradually worsened without a clear psychological explanation.
Mild intermittent frontal headaches in a previously headache-free individual.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe headache and acute vision loss, which may indicate pituitary apoplexy — haemorrhage or infarction into a prolactinoma.
Rapidly progressive visual field loss or double vision from optic chiasm compression by a rapidly growing macroprolactinoma.
Signs of pituitary crisis: severe headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, and haemodynamic instability requiring urgent imaging and endocrine assessment.
New-onset diabetes insipidus (excessive thirst, very high urine output) alongside hyperprolactinemia signs, suggesting major pituitary or hypothalamic disruption.

causes.description: A benign adenoma composed of prolactin-secreting lactotroph cells autonomously produces prolactin independent of physiological inhibition; it accounts for roughly 40% of all pituitary tumours and is by far the most common pathological cause of hyperprolactinemia, with microprolactinomas predominating in women and macroprolactinomas more frequent in men.

Antipsychotic drugs (haloperidol, risperidone, olanzapine), antiemetics (metoclopramide, domperidone), and certain antidepressants block pituitary dopamine D2 receptors, removing the tonic inhibitory brake on prolactin secretion; levels can reach 100–200 ng/mL and typically normalise within days of stopping the drug.

Insufficient thyroid hormone triggers a compensatory rise in thyrotropin-releasing hormone from the hypothalamus, which stimulates both TSH and prolactin secretion from the pituitary; treating the hypothyroidism with levothyroxine normalises prolactin without specific anti-prolactin therapy.

Tumours, granulomas, cysts, or trauma affecting the hypothalamus or pituitary stalk interrupt dopamine delivery to the anterior pituitary; the resulting 'stalk-effect' hyperprolactinemia rarely exceeds 150 ng/mL, a level that helps distinguish it from a macroprolactinoma.

The kidneys clear prolactin from the circulation; renal failure reduces clearance and allows prolactin to accumulate, with levels typically modestly elevated at two to three times the upper normal limit.

Thoracic surgery, herpes zoster affecting the chest, nipple piercing, and prolonged breast stimulation can trigger afferent neural signals that mimic suckling and stimulate prolactin release via the spinal cord.

riskFactors.description: Prolactinomas are up to five times more common in women aged 20–50 than in men of the same age, though the reason is not fully understood; in men, prolactinomas are often diagnosed later because symptom recognition is lower, and they tend to be larger at presentation.

Antipsychotics (particularly typical antipsychotics and risperidone), metoclopramide, and domperidone regularly cause clinically significant hyperprolactinemia; reviewing and potentially switching medication can resolve the condition.
Untreated or inadequately treated hypothyroidism continuously stimulates prolactin; this is a fully reversible cause with proper levothyroxine therapy.
Germline mutations in the MEN1 gene predispose to pituitary adenomas including prolactinomas; patients may develop tumours at a younger age and more frequently than the general population.
Reduced prolactin clearance in renal failure and altered hormone metabolism in cirrhosis both contribute to elevated levels.

Physiological prolactin elevation from stress or extreme physical training can occasionally be confused with pathological causes but does not produce the sustained suppression of the reproductive axis seen in true hyperprolactinemia.

affectedDemographics: Reproductive-age women aged 20–50, the largest affected group
Men of any age, in whom symptoms are often delayed and tumours are larger at diagnosis
Patients on long-term antipsychotic or antiemetic therapy
People with chronic kidney disease or renal failure
Patients with MEN1 syndrome or a family history of pituitary tumours
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a morning serum prolactin measurement; blood should be drawn without excessive venepuncture stress and away from recent nipple stimulation or vigorous exercise, which can transiently raise levels. A single result above 25 ng/mL in women or 20 ng/mL in men warrants at least one confirmatory measurement. Very high levels — generally above 250 ng/mL — are strongly suggestive of a macroprolactinoma, whereas levels below 100 ng/mL in the setting of a large pituitary mass may indicate stalk-effect hyperprolactinemia rather than a true secreting adenoma, a distinction with therapeutic implications. The workup includes thyroid function tests and renal function to exclude secondary causes, and a full medication review. In all confirmed cases, gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the pituitary is the imaging study of choice. If a macroadenoma is found, formal visual field testing by perimetry is performed to assess optic chiasm involvement. Additional pituitary hormone axes — growth hormone, ACTH/cortisol, TSH, LH/FSH — are tested when a macroprolactinoma is found, because tumour mass effect may impair other hormones. Macroprolactin (high-molecular-weight prolactin–immunoglobulin complexes) is biologically inactive but cross-reacts in standard assays; polyethylene glycol precipitation to test for macroprolactinemia should be done when prolactin elevation is asymptomatic, preventing unnecessary treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes and confirms elevated prolactin; the level helps gauge the likely cause — very high levels suggest a secreting adenoma, modestly elevated levels a secondary or medication cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm, ideally in the morning without prior breast stimulation or vigorous exercise; results take a few hours to one day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies and characterises pituitary adenomas, distinguishes microprolactinoma from macroprolactinoma, and detects stalk or hypothalamic lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30–45 minute scan inside an MRI machine, including an intravenous injection of gadolinium contrast; fine-cut pituitary sequences are used to detect lesions as small as 2–3 mm.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes primary hypothyroidism as the cause of elevated prolactin, which requires thyroid replacement rather than dopamine agonist treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the same blood draw; results within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies chronic kidney disease or cirrhosis as contributors to reduced prolactin clearance.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood panel taken at the same time as prolactin and thyroid tests.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bitemporal visual field loss from optic chiasm compression in patients with macroprolactinomas.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20–30 minute test at an ophthalmology clinic where you press a button each time you see a light flash in your peripheral visual field.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for hypopituitarism caused by mass effect from a macroprolactinoma; includes IGF-1, morning cortisol, LH, FSH, and testosterone or oestradiol.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A morning blood draw before 9 a.m., sometimes combined with stimulation tests if baseline cortisol is low.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies macroprolactinemia — biologically inactive large-molecular-weight prolactin — in asymptomatic patients with mildly elevated levels, avoiding unnecessary treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The same prolactin blood sample is processed with polyethylene glycol to precipitate large complexes before re-assaying; no additional blood draw required.

treatmentOverview: Dopamine agonists are the cornerstone of treatment for prolactinomas and for symptomatic idiopathic or medication-refractory hyperprolactinemia. Cabergoline, taken once or twice a week, normalises prolactin in approximately 85–90% of microprolactinoma patients and in 70–80% of macroprolactinoma patients, while also shrinking tumour volume by more than 25–50% in the majority. Bromocriptine, an older daily oral dopamine agonist, is preferred during fertility treatment because of the more extensive safety data in early pregnancy, though it causes more nausea and postural hypotension than cabergoline. For medication-induced hyperprolactinemia, the first step is reviewing whether the causative drug can be discontinued or substituted; if the psychiatric medication cannot be changed, a dopamine agonist can be added under careful supervision. Surgery — transsphenoidal resection — is reserved for prolactinomas that are medically resistant or intolerant, that cause acute visual loss or neurological deficit unresponsive to drug treatment, or that are large cystic tumours unlikely to shrink adequately on medication. Radiotherapy is rarely needed but can be considered for aggressive or recurrent tumours after surgery. Women with a microprolactinoma who wish to conceive can safely be treated with dopamine agonists through ovulation induction; the drug is usually stopped once pregnancy is confirmed, as most microprolactinomas do not grow significantly during pregnancy. Macroprolactinoma patients planning pregnancy require closer monitoring. Bone density should be assessed at baseline in patients with significant hypogonadism and monitored after treatment, as normalisation of sex hormones usually leads to bone recovery.

medicalTreatments.name: Cabergoline
medicalTreatments.description: A long-acting selective dopamine D2 receptor agonist taken orally once or twice a week; it is the first-line agent due to its superior efficacy, tolerability, and convenient dosing schedule. Gradual dose escalation minimises nausea and orthostatic hypotension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises prolactin in approximately 85–90% of microprolactinoma patients and 70–80% of macroprolactinomas; achieves clinically significant tumour shrinkage in over 70% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Bromocriptine
medicalTreatments.description: An older dopamine agonist taken daily or twice daily; it has a longer safety record in pregnancy and is often preferred when conception is planned. More nausea and hypotension than cabergoline require slow titration with food.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises prolactin in about 70–80% of patients and is effective for restoring ovulation and fertility in approximately 80% of anovulatory women with hyperprolactinemia.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine (for hypothyroidism-related cases)
medicalTreatments.description: When primary hypothyroidism is the cause, thyroid hormone replacement corrects the underlying TRH excess and normalises prolactin without any specific anti-prolactin therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reliably normalises prolactin within 4–8 weeks in nearly 100% of patients whose hyperprolactinemia is solely attributable to untreated hypothyroidism.

medicalTreatments.name: Medication switch or dose reduction (drug-induced cases)
medicalTreatments.description: Substituting the causative dopamine antagonist with an agent that lacks D2 receptor activity (e.g., switching from risperidone to quetiapine for psychiatric indications) typically resolves hyperprolactinemia within days to weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution achieved in the majority of drug-induced cases; prolactin typically falls by 50–80% or more after switching.

medicalTreatments.name: Transsphenoidal surgery
medicalTreatments.description: Endoscopic removal of the adenoma through a nostril; performed for drug-resistant or drug-intolerant prolactinomas, acute neurological deterioration, or patient preference. Outcomes are best in experienced pituitary surgery centres.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Biochemical remission in approximately 70–90% of microprolactinomas and 30–50% of macroprolactinomas, with recurrence rates of 15–50% at 5 years depending on tumour size.

surgicalOptions.name: Transsphenoidal adenomectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic or microscopic removal of the pituitary adenoma through the sphenoid sinus, preserving surrounding pituitary tissue; the standard surgical approach for prolactinoma when medical therapy fails or is contraindicated.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Biochemical cure in approximately 70–90% of microprolactinomas and 30–50% of macroprolactinomas at experienced centres; recurrence occurs in 15–50% within 5 years.

alternativeTreatments.name: Observation for microprolactinoma without symptoms
alternativeTreatments.description: In postmenopausal women or those who do not desire fertility, a microprolactinoma with modest prolactin elevation and no bothersome symptoms can be monitored with periodic prolactin checks and MRI rather than treated, since most do not enlarge.

alternativeTreatments.name: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Not a prolactin-lowering treatment, but essential adjunctive care for patients with hyperprolactinemia-induced bone loss, supporting bone density recovery alongside sex hormone restoration.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist should be involved once hyperprolactinemia is confirmed, because distinguishing a prolactinoma from drug-induced or secondary causes requires specialist interpretation, and because macroprolactinomas affecting vision or other pituitary hormones need multidisciplinary management with neurosurgery and ophthalmology. Reproductive endocrinology input is valuable when fertility is the primary concern.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an endocrinologist with specific expertise in pituitary disorders, ideally in a pituitary centre of excellence that holds multidisciplinary team meetings including neurosurgeons and ophthalmologists. If fertility is the goal, an endocrinologist who collaborates with reproductive medicine specialists ensures coordinated care. Ask whether the clinician monitors cardiac valve function in patients on long-term high-dose cabergoline, as recommended by recent guidance.
preventionStrategies: Review medications regularly; if a dopamine-antagonist drug is clinically necessary, use the lowest effective dose and monitor prolactin annually.
Treat primary hypothyroidism promptly to prevent the secondary rise in prolactin.
Screen for hyperprolactinemia in women with unexplained menstrual irregularity or infertility, enabling early treatment before bone loss accumulates.
In patients with known prolactinomas, adhere to dopamine agonist treatment and monitoring schedules to prevent tumour regrowth and hormone deficiency.
Genetic counselling for patients with MEN1 syndrome, in whom regular pituitary screening detects tumours early.
lifestyleModifications: Take cabergoline or bromocriptine with food to reduce nausea and postural hypotension during the dose-escalation phase.
Avoid excessive nipple or breast stimulation, which transiently raises prolactin and can affect monitoring measurements.
Maintain regular weight-bearing exercise to preserve bone density during the period of sex hormone deficiency before treatment normalises levels.
Adequate calcium (1,000–1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D (at least 800–1,000 IU/day) as adjunctive support for bone health.
Attend follow-up appointments consistently; prolactin checks every 6–12 months and periodic pituitary MRI allow early detection of tumour regrowth.

recommended: Calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens) to counteract bone loss from hypogonadism
Vitamin D from oily fish, eggs, or supplements to support calcium absorption
A balanced diet providing adequate zinc and selenium to support endocrine function
Taking dopamine agonist doses with a meal to reduce gastrointestinal side effects
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which impairs bone density and may affect pituitary function
Taking cabergoline or bromocriptine on an empty stomach, as this worsens nausea
Very high-fibre or low-fat restrictive diets that may further compromise oestrogen levels and bone health

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate weight-bearing exercise such as walking, jogging, and resistance training is actively encouraged to protect bone mineral density during the period of oestrogen or testosterone deficiency before prolactin normalises. Extreme endurance training or very low body-weight exercise regimes should be avoided, as they can independently suppress gonadotropin secretion and compound hypogonadism. Once prolactin is normalised and sex hormones recover, there are no specific exercise restrictions.
dailyManagement: Take dopamine agonist medication at the same time each dose cycle and always with food.
Record any headaches or visual changes and report them promptly, as these may indicate tumour expansion.
Keep a menstrual calendar (women) or note changes in libido and sexual function (men) as guides to treatment adequacy.
Carry a medication card noting the diagnosis and current treatment for emergency situations.
Attend pituitary MRI at the interval recommended by the treating endocrinologist — typically annually for macroprolactinomas and every 1–2 years for microprolactinomas.
prognosis: The outlook for hyperprolactinemia is generally excellent. Dopamine agonists normalise prolactin in 85–90% of patients with microprolactinomas, restore menstrual cycles and fertility, and reverse bone loss over 12–24 months of treatment. Macroprolactinomas also respond well to medical treatment in most cases, with tumour shrinkage documented in more than 70% of patients on cabergoline. After 2 years of stable normal prolactin on low-dose cabergoline, drug withdrawal results in sustained remission in approximately 21–69% of microprolactinoma patients — higher rates in those with smaller tumours and no visible adenoma on MRI at the time of withdrawal. Patients who require lifelong therapy tolerate it well, with the main monitoring concern being the association of high cumulative doses of cabergoline with cardiac valve fibrosis, though this is rare at the doses used for prolactinoma. Bone density lost during the hypogonadal period typically recovers substantially after sex hormone normalisation.
recoveryTimeline: Prolactin levels begin falling within days of starting a dopamine agonist and typically reach the normal range within 4–8 weeks at standard doses. Menstrual cycles usually return within 1–3 months, and ovulation may resume before the first normal period, so contraception should be considered if pregnancy is not planned. Bone density recovery is slower, generally improving over 12–24 months. For macroprolactinomas, tumour shrinkage is detectable on MRI within 3–6 months and can continue over 12–24 months of treatment. Visual field defects often begin improving within days to weeks of starting a dopamine agonist as tumour oedema resolves before measurable shrinkage occurs.
complications: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with bone loss — untreated hyperprolactinemia causes progressive osteopenia and osteoporosis from prolonged oestrogen or testosterone deficiency, raising fracture risk, particularly at the spine and hip.
Infertility — anovulation and suppressed spermatogenesis are reversible with treatment in the great majority of patients but can persist if hyperprolactinemia goes unrecognised for years.
Visual field loss — optic chiasm compression by a macroprolactinoma can cause permanent bitemporal hemianopia if not treated promptly; early dopamine agonist therapy usually reverses this.

Pituitary apoplexy — haemorrhage or infarction into a prolactinoma presents with sudden severe headache, diplopia, and visual loss and is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate imaging and specialist management.

Hypopituitarism — large macroprolactinomas can compress and damage normal pituitary cells, causing deficiencies of growth hormone, ACTH, TSH, and gonadotropins requiring long-term hormone replacement.

Cardiac valvulopathy — high cumulative cabergoline doses above those used for prolactinoma have been linked to valve fibrosis in Parkinson's disease patients; this risk at standard endocrine doses is considered very low but warrants periodic echocardiography in patients on high doses.

supportResources.name: Pituitary Foundation — Prolactinoma Information
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing detailed, patient-friendly information about prolactinoma diagnosis, treatment, and living with the condition.

supportResources.name: Pituitary Network Association
supportResources.description: US-based organisation offering peer support, educational resources, and a specialist directory for people with pituitary disorders including prolactinomas.

supportResources.name: NIH MedlinePlus — Hyperprolactinemia
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview of hyperprolactinemia causes, symptoms, and treatment from the U.S. National Library of Medicine.

sources.title: Melmed S et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyperprolactinemia: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline (2011)
Casanueva FF et al. Guidelines of the Pituitary Society for the Diagnosis and Management of Prolactinomas (2006)
Biller BM et al. Treatment of Prolactin-Secreting Macroadenomas with Cabergoline. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism, 1996
Dekkers OM et al. Dopamine Agonist Treatment and Remission in Prolactinomas: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. European Journal of Endocrinology, 2010
Glezer A, Bronstein MD. Prolactinomas. Endocrinology and Metabolism Clinics of North America, 2015
Schlechte JA. Clinical Practice: Prolactinoma. New England Journal of Medicine, 2003

confusedWithConditions.name: Acromegaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acromegaly results from excess growth hormone, causing bone and soft-tissue overgrowth and gigantism, whereas hyperprolactinemia causes reproductive and galactorrhea symptoms; both involve a pituitary adenoma but secrete different hormones.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary hypothyroidism can raise prolactin secondarily and cause menstrual irregularity, easily mistaken for a primary prolactinoma; TSH testing distinguishes the two, and thyroid replacement alone resolves hyperprolactinemia in hypothyroid cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic ovary syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PCOS causes irregular periods, androgen excess, and infertility from insulin resistance and ovarian dysfunction, not from elevated prolactin; serum prolactin is typically normal in PCOS, and pituitary MRI is unremarkable.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amenorrhea from other causes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Menstrual cessation from stress, extreme exercise, eating disorders, or premature ovarian insufficiency shares the presentation of absent periods but is not associated with galactorrhea or elevated prolactin.

relatedConditions.name: Acromegaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are functional pituitary adenomas; a mixed GH/prolactin co-secreting tumour can produce features of both, and screening for acromegaly is performed when a macroadenoma is found.

relatedConditions.name: Hypopituitarism
relatedConditions.relevance: Large macroprolactinomas can compress normal pituitary tissue and cause deficiencies of other pituitary hormones, making hypopituitarism a direct complication of untreated or inadequately treated prolactinoma.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic hypogonadism from untreated hyperprolactinemia is a direct and reversible cause of bone mineral density loss and vertebral fracture risk.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary adenoma
relatedConditions.relevance: A prolactinoma is the most common subtype of pituitary adenoma; other non-functioning or hormone-secreting adenomas in the same gland can co-exist or be found on imaging.

faqs.question: What causes high prolactin levels?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are a benign pituitary tumour called a prolactinoma, medications that block dopamine (antipsychotics, metoclopramide), and untreated hypothyroidism. Pregnancy and breastfeeding also raise prolactin naturally. Blood and pituitary MRI testing identifies the specific cause.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hyperprolactinemia?
faqs.answer: In women: irregular or absent periods, milky nipple discharge, and infertility. In men: reduced libido, erectile dysfunction, and decreased sperm count. Both sexes can develop headaches, bone loss, and low sex drive. Symptoms arise because excess prolactin suppresses sex hormones.

faqs.question: Can hyperprolactinemia cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Elevated prolactin suppresses the hormonal signals that trigger ovulation in women and sperm production in men. This is one of the most common correctable hormonal causes of infertility. Treating the underlying cause — usually with a dopamine agonist — restores fertility in approximately 80% of women.

faqs.question: How is hyperprolactinemia treated?
faqs.answer: Most cases are treated with a dopamine agonist — cabergoline (taken once or twice a week) or bromocriptine (daily). These drugs normalise prolactin in about 85–90% of patients with a small pituitary tumour and also shrink the tumour. Medication-induced cases resolve by adjusting the causative drug.

faqs.question: Is hyperprolactinemia serious?
faqs.answer: It is treatable and rarely life-threatening, but untreated it causes bone loss from low sex hormones, infertility, and — if a large tumour is present — vision problems. Early diagnosis and treatment prevent these complications and restore quality of life in the great majority of patients.

faqs.question: What is a prolactinoma?
faqs.answer: A prolactinoma is a benign tumour of the pituitary gland that produces excess prolactin. It is the most common cause of pathological hyperprolactinemia and the most frequent functional pituitary tumour overall. Small tumours (microprolactinomas under 10 mm) are treated successfully with oral tablets in most cases.

faqs.question: Does hyperprolactinemia go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Idiopathic hyperprolactinemia and some microprolactinomas do normalise spontaneously, particularly in postmenopausal women. However, treatment is generally recommended for patients with bothersome symptoms, infertility, or bone loss. The decision to observe rather than treat should be made with an endocrinologist.

faqs.question: Can hyperprolactinemia cause weight gain?
faqs.answer: Weight gain is not a direct symptom of hyperprolactinemia, but the associated low oestrogen or testosterone can alter body composition and energy regulation, and some patients on antipsychotic medications (a common cause) gain weight from those drugs rather than from the prolactin elevation itself.

faqs.question: How is a prolactinoma different from other pituitary tumours?
faqs.answer: A prolactinoma is specifically a tumour of prolactin-producing cells and is uniquely responsive to oral medication, making surgery rarely necessary. Other pituitary tumours may secrete growth hormone (acromegaly) or ACTH (Cushing disease) or produce no hormone at all, and they typically require surgery as first-line treatment.

faqs.question: Can men get hyperprolactinemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Men account for about 10–40% of prolactinoma cases. Their symptoms — reduced libido, erectile dysfunction, infertility — are less obvious than menstrual disruption, so diagnosis is often delayed. Men tend to have larger tumours at diagnosis because of later presentation.

faqs.question: Is cabergoline safe to take long term?
faqs.answer: Cabergoline at the doses used for prolactinoma (typically 0.25–2 mg per week) has a well-established safety record over decades. High doses used in Parkinson's disease have been linked to heart valve changes, but this risk is very low at standard endocrine doses. Periodic echocardiography may be recommended for patients on higher or prolonged doses.

faqs.question: Can I get pregnant if I have a prolactinoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dopamine agonist treatment restores ovulation in about 80% of women with prolactinoma-related infertility. Most guidelines recommend stopping the drug once pregnancy is confirmed for microprolactinoma patients. Women with macroprolactinomas require closer monitoring during pregnancy as some tumours can enlarge.

faqs.question: What prolactin level is concerning?
faqs.answer: Levels consistently above 25 ng/mL in women or 20 ng/mL in men warrant investigation. Levels above 100–200 ng/mL point strongly toward a prolactinoma. Very high levels above 250 ng/mL are almost always caused by a macroprolactinoma. Mildly elevated levels may be drug-related or due to macroprolactin.

faqs.question: Does high prolactin cause bone loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronically elevated prolactin suppresses oestrogen in women and testosterone in men, both of which are essential for maintaining bone density. Prolonged untreated hyperprolactinemia can cause osteoporosis. Treatment that normalises prolactin and sex hormones leads to meaningful bone density recovery over 1–2 years.

faqs.question: When should I have a pituitary MRI for high prolactin?
faqs.answer: An MRI of the pituitary with gadolinium contrast is recommended for all patients with confirmed pathological hyperprolactinemia once secondary causes (drugs, hypothyroidism) are excluded. It is the only test that can detect a pituitary adenoma and determine its size and proximity to the optic chiasm.

faqs.question: What happens if hyperprolactinemia is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Long-term untreated hyperprolactinemia leads to ongoing menstrual irregularity and infertility, progressive bone loss and fracture risk, continued sexual dysfunction, and — with large tumours — risk of vision loss and impairment of other pituitary hormones. These consequences are largely preventable with timely treatment.

faqs.question: Can stress raise prolactin levels?
faqs.answer: Acute physical stress (vigorous exercise, venepuncture discomfort, seizures) and psychological stress can transiently raise prolactin, but this effect is short-lived and does not cause the sustained reproductive suppression seen in true hyperprolactinemia. A confirmatory fasting morning sample minimises stress-related elevations.

faqs.question: Are there foods that lower prolactin?
faqs.answer: No food reliably lowers prolactin to a clinically meaningful degree. A balanced diet rich in calcium and vitamin D is important to protect bone health during treatment, but dietary changes do not substitute for medical therapy. Some herbal preparations are marketed for prolactin reduction but lack clinical trial evidence.

faqs.question: How often should prolactin be checked during treatment?
faqs.answer: Typically every 3–6 months during the dose-titration phase, then every 6–12 months once prolactin is stable in the normal range. If treatment is stopped after a period of normalised levels, more frequent monitoring resumes to detect recurrence early.

faqs.question: Can hyperprolactinemia cause depression or anxiety?
faqs.answer: Low oestrogen and testosterone from prolactin-mediated hypogonadism are associated with low mood, irritability, and reduced motivation. These mood symptoms often improve significantly once prolactin normalises and sex hormones recover, distinguishing them from primary psychiatric disorders.

metaDescription: Hyperprolactinemia raises prolactin and disrupts periods, fertility and sex hormones. Learn causes, dopamine agonist treatment and when to see a specialist.
